data.epi_content:
# A Grave Mistake

I made a terrible mistake.

I burned a girl to death.

That itself wasn't the mistake.

It's what happened afterward that has put me in quite a difficult position.

In any case, I was ultimately the one who drove the girl to that situation.

Despite knowing her circumstances, her mental state, and how she had been living recently, I didn't offer a single helping hand.

If someone asked how I feel about it, what should I say?

The first time I committed a similar act, my hands and feet trembled, and I couldn't eat properly for days. Now, I just feel a bit melancholic, and that's it.

I've grown accustomed to it.

That's what I hate the most.

If I were to make excuses, it was for work.

Though extremely unlikely, there was concern that several nobles might gather around House Mecklenburg to rebel and possibly overthrow the country.

The Emperor wished for it, and so did I.

Regardless of my abilities, this kind of work doesn't seem to suit me.

The middle-aged man whose death was widely reported as an accident never had the capacity to start a rebellion.

He might suddenly go mad and raise an army, but he would never succeed.

He managed his territory neatly and drew considerable profits from plantations in occupied territories, but that was the extent of his abilities.

He had neither ambition nor the capability to achieve it.

He was just an ordinary man who loved his children, especially his daughter.

But his eldest son was dangerous.

A young man overflowing with ambition, with a background second only to the imperial family.

He was well-respected, and his abilities weren't far behind the Crown Prince's...

Yes, he was too outstanding for the imperial family to tolerate.

Fortunately, my father, though ignorant about governing the state or managing neighboring countries, is a historically ruthless ruler who excels at consolidating power. When I told him about this young man full of potential, he readily helped me dispose of him.

As the time for disposal approached, I found myself regretting it.

Not because of any human reluctance to eliminate such a person, but because of the waste of such excellent human resources.

Just as the Duke Mecklenburg's grandfather was the first among many families to invest his entire fortune in ships to pioneer the dark, undeveloped continent and breathe new life into the Empire, the Duke's eldest son also seemed capable of discovering or creating something new.

Had that happened, it would have greatly benefited the Empire, which was slowly losing strength and dying under its massive weight.

But he was dangerous.

This enormous country's only enemy is itself.

Therefore, the most dangerous person to the Empire was the eldest son of House Mecklenburg.

Even if he was a valuable person, these weren't chaotic times, nor was the power structure so unstable that we needed to embrace every talented individual.

If a protruding hard stone gets stuck and won't come out, it's enough to bring a hammer and break it.
data.epi_content2:
If a mere ducal house could breathe life into the Empire, then I could do it too.

Though my abilities might be inferior to his, my power and resources were far superior.

If I failed, it would be enough to designate some neighboring country as heretics and burn them all to the ground.

So I removed him.

Along with several of his followers.

I attached a suitable reason that no one would believe.

A lie that he was preparing a plot to assassinate His Majesty the Emperor with explosives purchased using imperial capital that he had embezzled while conducting business.

In reality, his servant had merely purchased explosives for use in the mines.

Anyway, he's been dead for a long time now.

To completely bring down House Mecklenburg and devour it afterward, I ordered my vassals to stir up various troubles.

And then I heard some rather absurd responses.

Without doing anything, rumors spread about the Duchess consorting with not one but six men, and then a dragon appeared from somewhere and burned down the main residence of House Mecklenburg. Fanatics occupied the mining city, declaring they would establish their own country, and what little remained of the ashes fell into the hands of an incompetent, incapable bastard.

I had only killed the eldest son, yet all this happened.

Duke Mecklenburg, despairing of his life, chose to end it.

Upon hearing this news, I laughed for a long time in my room.

It felt like picking up a pretty stone on the street only to discover it was a gem.

Then I frowned for a long time in self-loathing, spitting as I filled my burning insides with cigar smoke.

I killed all the fanatics who occupied the mining city, and labeled all the residents, merchants, and miners as heretics corrupted by evil and killed them too.

It was natural that they would harbor resentment toward the imperial family, given their wealth and the situation that made it obvious the Emperor had killed the Duke.

Only fools would ignore such a perfect pretext.

People are abundant in this country.

There were plenty of replacements for them.

I let the dragon feast sufficiently after burning down the mansion before sending knights to chase it away.

Well-fed animals, whether human or beast, aren't usually that ferocious.

It didn't seem worthwhile to waste valuable human resources on a pointless fight.

I left the Duke's half-wit alone.

He was the perfect candidate to tarnish the still-prominent family name, wasting all the remaining assets on useless things, always drunk and causing trouble.

As for the Duchess, well, her husband died with one of his mistresses, so what could she do without money?

Despite her age, her face still looked like a newly married woman's, so she could either end up in a brothel or make the same choice as the Duke.

I feel nauseated by how calmly and habitually these thoughts come to me.

Whenever that happens, I smoke strong tobacco to calm myself and spend time sitting on a comfortable sofa, but I couldn't resolve this feeling of self-loathing.

During this time, I met the daughter of Duke Mecklenburg.

I went to a specialty shop to buy some tobacco leaves, and she was there selecting tea.

She must have lived a life where she could have anything she wanted before.

Was it momentary compassion?

I bought both the tea leaves I liked and the ones she was contemplating, and casually spoke to her.

Not as Johannes, but as Crown Prince Johannes, the head of this massive hostage warehouse for students.
data.epi_content3:
I told her if she wanted to die, she should go into her room and die quietly.

And I attached a shadow to her.

From what I heard, she seemed to have gone mad.

And since madmen are unpredictable and should be dealt with preemptively... was it misplaced compassion?

I decided to leave her alone.

But one day, I heard that Vivian had taken her in, and instead of letting her commit suicide, kept her locked in a room and always by her side.

As time went on, the precious daughter of House Mecklenburg withered mentally before physically.

So I killed her.

I gave her a suitable drug and a powder that would create a dramatic death.

A student committed self-immolation in the middle of the Academy.

The story was that she was heartbroken and despairing over her family's downfall, but I manipulated public opinion to spread rumors that the eldest son of House Strelitz had cruelly played with her and then discarded her after taking her body.

The effect was dramatic.

Though he didn't seem to care much.

Thanks to this, I gained enough leverage to grab and shake, if not the neck, then at least a hand or foot of House Strelitz.

And that was the mistake.

The man who loved his family so much has locked himself in his room and spent all day reading books since Erica died.

Whatever research he's doing, he's surrounded his room with a magical barrier.

As time passed, he eventually conducted human experiments within the Academy to resurrect Erica, and invited Vivian to join him.

Of course, she refused and fought against him, winning.

But according to the shadow, she couldn't bring herself to kill him and let him go.

Much time has passed since then.

Vivian accepted my recommendation, graduated from the Academy, and became an imperial magician. The Emperor went mad, so I personally confined him to a tower and became Emperor myself.

It was a fair deal - I just had to send him meals and women.

And since then, I've lived forgetting about the girl in my memories.

Recalling her doesn't bring up particularly pleasant memories anyway.

It was a grave mistake.

Looking back now, it seems to have started with killing that eldest son.

The population of Strelitz, which I had planned to devour someday, has been halved, and its pride, the knights, have all been sacrificed to demons.

Also, a corrupt kingdom I had planned to absorb later has collapsed.

They say not a single person or blade of grass grows there now.

The mining city, port, and shipyard in Mecklenburg have all burned to nothing.

The fanatics I thought I had uprooted have resurfaced and are indiscriminately killing people in the surrounding territories.

All caused by one person.

The bookworm I thought was stupid has torn the country in half just trying to revive one woman.

Emperor seems to be a profession that doesn't suit my aptitude.

So much so that I want to quit right now.
